events.title: National Revolutionary Party (PNR) Founded
events.detail: Plutarco Elías Calles founded the Partido Nacional Revolucionario in Querétaro as a mechanism to institutionalize the Mexican Revolution and end caudillo-based succession conflicts. The party brought regional military chiefs and revolutionary factions under a single organizational umbrella. This ended the era of military strongmen (caudillos) and began the era of institutionalized one-party rule.

sources.name: Josefina Zoraida Vázquez, El Colegio de México

events.title: First PNR Presidential Election — Ortiz Rubio vs. Vasconcelos
events.detail: Pascual Ortiz Rubio (PNR) was declared the winner over José Vasconcelos with 93% of the vote amid widespread fraud allegations. Vasconcelos led a genuine popular movement but fraud was systematic. This election established the template of PRI-managed electoral outcomes that would persist for 70 years. Vasconcelos refused to accept the results and went into exile.

sources.name: Enrique Krauze, La Presidencia Imperial, Tusquets

events.title: Lázaro Cárdenas Elected President
events.detail: Lázaro Cárdenas del Río was elected PNR president with overwhelming support. Initially seen as a Calles loyalist, Cárdenas broke sharply with the Jefe Máximo and pursued a radical agrarian and labor program. His election marked the beginning of the most transformative presidency in post-revolutionary Mexico, redistributing more land than any predecessor.

sources.name: Friedrich Katz, The Life and Times of Pancho Villa, Stanford
Alan Knight, Mexico: From the Beginning to the Spanish Conquest, Cambridge

events.title: Calles Expelled — End of the Maximato
events.detail: President Cárdenas ordered Plutarco Elías Calles expelled from Mexico and deported to the United States, ending the 'Maximato' period in which Calles controlled three puppet presidents (1928–1936). The expulsion demonstrated Cárdenas's political strength and transformed the PNR from Calles's instrument into the state's organizational arm. It was the decisive consolidation of presidential authority over the party.

events.title: Oil Nationalization — PEMEX Created
events.detail: President Cárdenas nationalized the oil industry, expropriating 17 foreign oil companies (Standard Oil, Royal Dutch Shell) after they defied a Supreme Court labor ruling. The nationalization was the most popular act of Mexican statism — citizens donated jewelry to pay the compensation. Petróleos Mexicanos (PEMEX) was created as the national oil company. March 18 became a national holiday.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, March 18, 1938
Lorenzo Meyer, Su Majestad Británica contra la Revolución Mexicana, El Colegio de México

events.title: PNR Transformed into PRM — Corporatist Structure Formalized
events.detail: Cárdenas restructured the PNR into the Partido de la Revolución Mexicana (PRM) with four sectoral pillars: peasant (CNC), labor (CTM), popular (CNOP), and military. This corporatist structure integrated key social groups directly into the state party, ensuring electoral mobilization through organized sectors. The military sector was eliminated in 1940 under Ávila Camacho.

sources.name: Juan Molinar Horcasitas, El tiempo de la legitimidad, Cal y Arena

events.title: Ávila Camacho Elected — End of Cardenismo
events.detail: Manuel Ávila Camacho defeated Juan Andrew Almazán in a contested election that saw unprecedented violence and fraud allegations. Almazán — a conservative general — refused to accept the results and considered a coup but ultimately desisted. The election marked the end of Cárdenas's radical phase and the beginning of a conservative turn that would characterize PRI's subsequent rule.

sources.name: Roderic Ai Camp, Mexican Political Biographies, University of Texas

events.title: PRM Renamed PRI — First Civilian President
events.detail: The PRM was renamed Partido Revolucionario Institucional (PRI) under Miguel Alemán, who also became Mexico's first civilian president in the post-revolutionary era. The renaming reflected the party's claim to have institutionalized the revolution and its shift toward technocratic governance. Alemán's presidency began Mexico's industrialization-first development model, favoring capital over labor.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, January 18, 1946
Luis Medina Peña, Hacia el nuevo estado: México, 1920–2000, FCE

events.title: Alemán Inaugurated — Pro-Business PRI Model
events.detail: Miguel Alemán Valdés inaugurated a new PRI model that prioritized industrialization, infrastructure (highways, dams, UNAM campus), and private investment over agrarian reform. His presidency established the technocratic PRI tradition. Corruption became institutionalized under Alemán — the term 'alemanismo' became synonymous with corruption among subsequent generations.

sources.name: Roderic Ai Camp, Politics in Mexico, Oxford University Press
Enrique Krauze, Mexico: Biography of Power, HarperCollins

events.title: Women Gain Full Voting Rights
events.detail: Mexico granted women full political rights including the vote and eligibility for national office under President Adolfo Ruiz Cortines. Mexico was relatively late among Latin American nations — Argentina granted women's suffrage in 1947. The reform expanded the PRI's electoral base; women, organized through CNOP, became a key corporatist sector that PRI mobilized through official women's organizations.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, October 17, 1953
Esperanza Tuñón Pablos, Mujeres que se organizan, UNAM

events.title: Adolfo López Mateos Elected President
events.detail: López Mateos won with 90.4% in what critics called a managed election. His presidency (1958–1964) represented the high water mark of 'stabilizing development' with 6% annual GDP growth, stable peso, and major social investments including ISSSTE and the nationalization of the electric industry. He declared himself 'of the extreme left within the Constitution' while suppressing independent unions.

sources.name: Daniel Cosío Villegas, El sistema político mexicano, Joaquín Mortiz

events.title: Nationalization of Mexico's Electric Industry
events.detail: President López Mateos purchased the shares of foreign electric companies (American and Foreign Power, Mexican Light and Power) for $70 million, nationalizing the electrical grid. The move was extremely popular and followed the logic of economic nationalism established by Cárdenas's 1938 oil nationalization. CFE (Comisión Federal de Electricidad) took over operations as a state monopoly.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, September 27, 1960

events.title: Gustavo Díaz Ordaz Elected President
events.detail: Díaz Ordaz won with 88.8% under PRI's managed electoral system. Interior Minister under López Mateos, he was known as a hardliner and authoritarian. His presidency (1964–1970) combined economic growth with political repression. The 1968 Olympics in Mexico City were to be his showcase; instead, the Tlatelolco massacre became his legacy.

events.title: PPS and PARM — PRI's Controlled Opposition
events.detail: The 1964 elections saw the PPS (Partido Popular Socialista) and PARM (Partido Auténtico de la Revolución Mexicana) participate as satellite parties that endorsed the PRI candidate. These 'loyal opposition' parties received congressional seats via a 1963 proportional representation law but posed no genuine challenge. This system of controlled opposition maintained democratic appearances while ensuring PRI dominance.

sources.name: Juan Molinar Horcasitas, El tiempo de la legitimidad, Cal y Arena

events.title: Tlatelolco Massacre — The Night of Tlatelolco
events.detail: Military and police forces opened fire on student protesters gathered at Plaza de las Tres Culturas in Tlatelolco, Mexico City, 10 days before the Olympics. The government used the covert Olympic Battalion, which provoked chaos before the Army fired. Official death toll was 30–40; independent estimates range from 200–300. The bodies were removed overnight. Journalist Elena Poniatowska documented survivors' testimonies in La Noche de Tlatelolco (1971).

sources.name: Elena Poniatowska, La Noche de Tlatelolco, Era

events.title: Echeverría's 'Democratic Opening' — Apertura Democrática
events.detail: Luis Echeverría became president promising a 'democratic opening' after the Tlatelolco trauma. He released political prisoners, allowed some press criticism, and promoted a Third World foreign policy. However, his government also carried out the June 10, 1971 Corpus Christi massacre of student protesters and conducted the 'dirty war' against guerrilla movements, disappearing hundreds of activists.

sources.name: Julio Scherer García & Carlos Monsiváis, Tiempo de saber, Aguilar

events.title: Corpus Christi Massacre — 'El Halconazo'
events.detail: A paramilitary group known as 'Los Halcones' (The Falcons), trained and organized by Mexico City authorities, attacked student protesters on Corpus Christi day. At least 25–33 students were killed. Evidence linked the attack to Interior Minister Mario Moya Palencia and Mexico City Regent Alfonso Martínez Domínguez, who resigned. Echeverría denied knowledge — a claim that was later contradicted by the FEMOSPP investigation.

events.title: Mexico's Dirty War — Estado's Campaign Against Guerrillas
events.detail: From the early 1970s through the 1980s, the Mexican state conducted a systematic campaign of forced disappearances, torture, and extrajudicial killings against leftist guerrilla groups (Liga Comunista 23 de Septiembre, Lucio Cabañas's Partido de los Pobres) and political dissidents. The FEMOSPP documented over 700 forced disappearances. The Guerrero and Oaxaca mountains saw the most intense operations. No senior officials were ever convicted.

sources.name: Human Rights Watch, Mexico's Dirty War, 2002

events.title: Political Reform (LOPPE) — Left Parties Legalized
events.detail: Interior Minister Jesús Reyes Heroles under President López Portillo implemented the Ley Federal de Organizaciones Políticas y Procesos Electorales (LOPPE), legalizing the Communist Party (PCM) and other left parties for the first time. The reform granted conditional registration and proportional representation seats in Congress, allowing limited political pluralism while maintaining PRI control. It was the first significant electoral opening.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, December 28, 1977
Soledad Loaeza, El Partido Acción Nacional, FCE

events.title: Mexico Debt Crisis — De la Madrid's Austerity
events.detail: Mexico announced it could not service its $80 billion external debt on August 12, 1982, triggering the Latin American debt crisis. The peso was devalued by 70%. President López Portillo nationalized the banks in his last months in office. His successor, Miguel de la Madrid, implemented IMF-mandated austerity that slashed real wages by 40% over the decade. The crisis destroyed PRI's economic legitimacy.

sources.name: Sebastian Edwards, The Debt Crisis in Latin America, World Bank

events.title: López Portillo Nationalizes Banks
events.detail: In his final presidential address, José López Portillo announced the nationalization of Mexico's private banking system — a populist response to the debt crisis and capital flight. He wept publicly and blamed 'a group of Mexicans' who 'plundered the country.' The nationalization was partially reversed under de la Madrid (1985) and fully under Salinas (1990). The episode became a symbol of economic mismanagement.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, September 1, 1982
Carlos Tello Macías, La Nacionalización de la Banca, Siglo XXI

events.title: Mexico City Earthquake — Civil Society Awakens
events.detail: An 8.1 magnitude earthquake killed at least 9,500 people in Mexico City and left 300,000 homeless. The government's disorganized response led civil society to self-organize without the state — a historic turning point. Brigades of volunteers in neighborhoods like Tlatelolco, Roma, and Tepito became the seeds of NGO culture and independent civic life. Many scholars date modern Mexican civil society to this moment.

sources.name: Elena Poniatowska, Nada, nadie: las voces del temblor, Era

events.title: Chihuahua Gubernatorial Election — PAN Alleges Fraud
events.detail: PAN candidate Francisco Barrio was widely believed to have won the Chihuahua governorship against PRI's Fernando Baeza, but PRI was declared the winner. The fraud triggered mass protests and a PAN campaign of civil disobedience including a bridge blockade. The U.S. government criticized the results. The episode radicalized the PAN and foreshadowed the 1988 crisis.

sources.name: Soledad Loaeza, El Partido Acción Nacional, FCE

events.title: Corriente Democrática — PRI Fractures
events.detail: Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas, Porfirio Muñoz Ledo, and allies formed the Corriente Democrática inside PRI, demanding democratic internal elections for the 1988 presidential candidate rather than the dedazo (finger-pointing) tradition. When Salinas was chosen through the traditional process, Cárdenas broke with PRI and ran as an independent coalition candidate — the first credible challenge to PRI's presidential monopoly in decades.

sources.name: Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas, Sobre mis pasos, Aguilar

events.title: "Se Cayó el Sistema" — 1988 Presidential Election
events.detail: Mexico's contested presidential election saw the federal electoral commission's computer system 'crash' as early returns showed Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas leading Carlos Salinas de Gortari. When the system 'resumed,' Salinas was winning. PRI's Manuel Bartlett Díaz controlled the process. Salinas was officially declared winner with 50.7%. The phrase 'se cayó el sistema' entered the Mexican political lexicon as a metaphor for electoral fraud.

sources.name: Rolando Cordera et al., La jornada electoral, Era

events.title: PRD Founded — Cárdenas Builds the Left
events.detail: Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas united the coalition that had run against Salinas into the Partido de la Revolución Democrática (PRD), merging the PSUM, PMS, and Corriente Democrática. The PRD positioned itself as the democratic left, combining social democracy with Mexican nationalism. It became the dominant left party for the next 25 years, winning Mexico City and multiple states before being eclipsed by Morena.

sources.name: Comité Ejecutivo Nacional PRD, Declaración de Principios
Kathleen Bruhn, Taking on Goliath: The Emergence of a New Left Party, Penn State

events.title: PAN Wins Baja California — First Opposition Governor
events.detail: Ernesto Ruffo Appel became Mexico's first opposition state governor when PAN won Baja California. President Salinas chose to accept the result — breaking with decades of PRI fraud — in part to demonstrate democratic credentials ahead of NAFTA negotiations. The victory opened a crack in PRI's monopoly on state power and launched a wave of competitive state elections throughout the 1990s.

sources.name: Comisión Estatal Electoral Baja California, 1989 results
Soledad Loaeza, El Partido Acción Nacional, FCE

events.title: IFE Created — Electoral Institution Established
events.detail: The Instituto Federal Electoral (IFE) was created in 1990 to replace the self-managed electoral system in which the Interior Ministry (controlled by PRI) ran all elections. Initially still controlled by the government, IFE was transformed by successive reforms (1993, 1994, 1996) into a genuinely autonomous body. It certified the 2000 Fox victory and ran Mexico's elections until being renamed INE in 2014.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, Code of Federal Institutions and Electoral Procedures, 1990
José Woldenberg, Historia mínima de la transición democrática en México, El Colegio de México

events.title: NAFTA Signed by Mexico, U.S., and Canada
events.detail: Presidents Salinas, Bush, and Prime Minister Mulroney signed the North American Free Trade Agreement, eliminating tariffs on trade over 15 years. For Mexico, it represented Salinas's bet on export-led growth and full integration with the North American economy. Opponents — led by Cárdenas's PRD and later the EZLN — argued it would devastate Mexican agriculture and sovereignty. NAFTA took effect January 1, 1994.

sources.name: U.S. Department of State, NAFTA text

events.title: Electoral Reform — IFE Gains Full Autonomy
events.detail: A landmark multi-party electoral reform removed the Interior Minister from the IFE board and created a fully independent council of nine citizen councillors elected by Congress. The reform also allowed the federal district (Mexico City) to elect its own head of government for the first time. Proportional representation was expanded. This reform is credited as the institutional foundation of Mexico's democratic transition.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, November 22, 1996 electoral reform
José Woldenberg, La construcción de la democracia, Plaza y Janés

events.title: EZLN Uprising in Chiapas — Zapatista Revolution
events.detail: On the first day of NAFTA, the Ejército Zapatista de Liberación Nacional (EZLN) captured San Cristóbal de las Casas and five other Chiapas towns, declaring war on the Mexican government. Subcomandante Marcos articulated indigenous rights, anti-NAFTA critique, and democratic demands. A ceasefire was declared January 12. The uprising shocked the world and forced the government into unprecedented negotiations, leading to the 1996 San Andrés Accords.

sources.name: John Womack Jr., Rebellion in Chiapas, New Press

events.title: Luis Donaldo Colosio Assassinated in Tijuana
events.detail: PRI's presidential candidate Luis Donaldo Colosio was shot and killed at a campaign rally in Tijuana. The gunman, Mario Aburto Martínez, was immediately apprehended. Official investigations concluded Aburto acted alone, but conspiracy theories — implicating PRI hard-liners, the Salinas circle, and narcos — were never fully debunked. Ernesto Zedillo replaced Colosio as PRI candidate.

sources.name: Jorge Fernández Menéndez, Nadie supo nada: la verdadera historia del asesinato de Colosio, Grijalbo

events.title: Ernesto Zedillo Elected President
events.detail: Ernesto Zedillo won the first widely monitored presidential election with 50.2%, the lowest PRI total in history at that point. International observers certified the election as competitive, though not fully free and fair. PAN's Diego Fernández de Cevallos and PRD's Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas competed seriously. IFE credentialized voters for the first time with photo ID cards. The election was a step toward competitive democracy.

events.title: Tequila Crisis — Peso Devaluation
events.detail: Three weeks after Zedillo's inauguration, Mexico devalued the peso by 15%, triggering capital flight and a financial crisis. The peso lost half its value within months. GDP contracted 6.2% in 1995. The U.S. organized a $50 billion rescue package. The crisis deepened poverty and demonstrated the fragility of Salinas's neoliberal model. It also severely damaged PRI's economic credibility.

sources.name: Sebastian Edwards, Crisis and Reform in Latin America, World Bank

events.title: Cárdenas Wins Mexico City — First Elected HoG
events.detail: Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas won the first direct election for Mexico City's head of government with 48% of the vote, following the 1996 reform that ended the appointed regency system. This was the PRD's greatest electoral triumph to that point. Mexico City became a progressive policy laboratory under Cárdenas and successors López Obrador and Marcelo Ebrard.

events.title: PRI Loses Congressional Majority — Divided Government
events.detail: In the same 1997 midterm elections, PRI lost its absolute majority in the Chamber of Deputies for the first time since 1929. PAN and PRD together held enough seats to block PRI legislation. This created Mexico's first divided government and required PRI to negotiate with opposition parties. It proved a political turning point toward genuine pluralism.

events.title: Vicente Fox Wins Presidency — End of PRI Hegemony
events.detail: Vicente Fox Quesada of PAN won Mexico's presidency with 42.5% against PRI's Francisco Labastida (36.1%) and PRD's Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas (17%). After 71 years of uninterrupted PRI rule, Ernesto Zedillo accepted the result and delivered power peacefully. Fox's victory — carried by a coalition 'Alianza por el Cambio' — was certified by the IFE in the most transparent election in Mexican history to that point. Zedillo called Fox immediately to congratulate him.

sources.name: Jorge Domínguez & Chappell Lawson, Mexico's Pivotal Democratic Election, Stanford

era: PAN Governments — Alternation and Security Crisis (2000–2012)

events.title: Fox Inaugurated — 'Today, Mexico Changes'
events.detail: Vicente Fox was inaugurated as Mexico's first non-PRI president in 71 years in a ceremony marked by euphoria and hope. He arrived wearing a traditional guayabera and a belt buckle with the image of the Virgin of Guadalupe, symbolizing his mix of western modernity and Mexican traditionalism. He promised transparency, rule of law, and to solve the Chiapas conflict 'in 15 minutes' — a promise he failed to keep.

sources.name: Presidencia de la República, Diario Oficial

events.title: FEMOSPP Created — Investigation of Dirty War
events.detail: Fox created the Fiscalía Especial para Movimientos Sociales y Políticos del Pasado (FEMOSPP) to investigate the dirty war disappearances, Tlatelolco, and Corpus Christi massacres. The office filed nine indictments including against former President Luis Echeverría for genocide. All charges were ultimately dismissed. The office was dissolved in 2006, leaving victims without justice but producing an important historical archive.

sources.name: Kate Doyle, NSA Mexico project, National Security Archive

events.title: AMLO Wins Mexico City Head of Government
events.detail: Andrés Manuel López Obrador won Mexico City's head of government election succeeding Cárdenas. His government implemented pioneering social programs — pensions for elderly, scholarships, loans for small business — that would become national templates. He governed with 58% approval, making Mexico City the platform for his national presidential ambition. He introduced the 'Segundo Piso' elevated highway project.

events.title: Desafuero of AMLO — Failed Political Prosecution
events.detail: The Fox government and PRI supported a congressional vote to strip AMLO of his immunity (desafuero) over a land dispute, which would have barred him from running for president. The move backfired spectacularly — mass protests filled the Zócalo, AMLO's popularity surged, and Fox was forced to back down. The episode confirmed AMLO as the leading opposition figure and reinforced his narrative as a victim of elite conspiracy.

sources.name: Cámara de Diputados, congressional record, April 2005

events.title: Calderón Wins Disputed Election by 0.56%
events.detail: Felipe Calderón (PAN) was declared winner over AMLO (PRD-led coalition) by 243,934 votes — 0.56% — after the IFE's controversial vote count. AMLO refused to accept the result, declared himself 'legitimate president,' set up a tent city in Paseo de la Reforma for weeks, and held a counter-inauguration in the Zócalo. The TEPJF upheld the result. The crisis left Mexico polarized for over a decade.

sources.name: Jorge Castañeda & Marco Morales, Leftovers: Tales of the Latin American Left, Routledge

events.title: Calderón Launches Military Drug War
events.detail: Ten days after his inauguration, Calderón deployed 6,500 soldiers to his home state of Michoacán, marking the beginning of Mexico's military-led war on organized crime. He presented himself in military garb at the ceremony. The strategy — targeting cartel leadership through military operations — escalated throughout his term. Homicides rose from 8,867 in 2007 to 27,213 in 2011 before declining slightly.

sources.name: Secretaría de la Defensa Nacional press releases, December 2006
Alejandro Poiré & María Teresa Martínez, Proceso magazine

events.title: Ciudad Juárez — World's Most Violent City
events.detail: Ciudad Juárez recorded 3,111 homicides in 2010 — the highest rate of any city in the world — as the Sinaloa and Juárez cartels fought for control of the key border crossing with military deployed between them. Femicides, massacres at youth gatherings, and targeted killings of journalists characterized the crisis. The humanitarian catastrophe became a symbol of Calderón's drug war's unintended consequences.

sources.name: Charles Bowden, Murder City: Ciudad Juárez and the Global Economy's New Killing Fields

events.title: #YoSoy132 Student Movement
events.detail: The #YoSoy132 student movement emerged when 131 students from Iberoamericana Universidad challenged Peña Nieto's version of events during a campus visit — he had claimed they were political infiltrators. The students posted a video of themselves with IDs. The movement expanded into a mass protest against media manipulation (Televisa's coverage of Peña Nieto), and became one of Mexico's first major social-media-driven political movements.

events.title: Peña Nieto Elected — PRI Returns to Power
events.detail: Enrique Peña Nieto (PRI) won the presidency with 38.2%, defeating AMLO (25.9%) and PAN's Josefina Vázquez Mota (25.4%). PRI's return after 12 years was aided by massive media coverage — Televisa was accused of systematically favoring Peña Nieto — and the party's organizational capacity in rural areas. AMLO again rejected the results but with less support than 2006.

events.title: Pacto por México — Multi-Party Reform Agenda
events.detail: One day after his inauguration, Peña Nieto unveiled the Pacto por México — a 95-point reform agreement signed by PRI, PAN, and PRD. The pact committed to energy reform, education reform, telecom reform (addressing Slim's monopoly), fiscal reform, and financial reform. It was celebrated internationally as proof Mexico could govern effectively across party lines. Implementation produced mixed results.

sources.name: Presidencia de la República, Pacto por México text, December 2012

events.title: Energy Reform — PEMEX Opened to Private Capital
events.detail: Constitutional reforms ended PEMEX's 75-year monopoly on Mexican oil and gas, allowing production-sharing agreements and licenses with private companies for the first time since 1938. The reform was presented as modernization to reverse declining production (from 3.4 Mbpd in 2004 to 2.5 Mbpd in 2013). Critics called it 'the second oil expropriation in reverse.' AMLO promised to reverse it upon taking power.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, December 20, 2013
George Baker, Mexico's Petroleum Sector, PennWell

events.title: INE Replaces IFE — Electoral Reform
events.detail: A constitutional reform replaced IFE with the Instituto Nacional Electoral (INE), centralizing electoral administration at the federal level including state elections. The reform created the OPLE (local electoral organs) and gave INE the power to directly organize state elections when local bodies fail. Critics argued the reform centralized power; advocates said it standardized quality. Lorenzo Córdova became the controversial INE president.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, April 10, 2014
Lorenzo Córdova, INE institutional press release

events.title: 43 Ayotzinapa Students Forcibly Disappeared
events.detail: On the night of September 26–27, 2014, 43 male students from the Raúl Isidro Burgos Rural Teachers' College in Ayotzinapa, Guerrero, were intercepted by Iguala police while traveling to Mexico City. They were handed to the Guerreros Unidos cartel. Attorney General Murillo Karam announced a 'historical truth' that they were incinerated at a dump in Cocula — a conclusion rejected by the GIEI (Interdisciplinary Group of Independent Experts). No bodies were confirmed found.

events.title: Casa Blanca Scandal — Peña Nieto's Corruption
events.detail: Investigative journalist Carmen Aristegui and her team revealed that First Lady Angélica Rivera owned a 7 million dollar mansion ('La Casa Blanca') through a company linked to Grupo Higa, a major government contractor. Peña Nieto denied wrongdoing and Aristegui was fired from MVS Radio — a move widely attributed to government pressure. The scandal devastated Peña Nieto's approval ratings.

sources.name: Carmen Aristegui, MVS Noticias investigation, November 2014
Animal Político, 'La Casa Blanca de Enrique Peña Nieto'

events.title: Panama Papers — Mexico Officials Named
events.detail: The ICIJ's Panama Papers leak implicated multiple Mexican officials and political figures in offshore accounts, including former PRI governors and businessmen linked to the party. The revelations reinforced the corruption narrative around PRI. Several governors were subsequently investigated — Javier Duarte (Veracruz), Roberto Borge (Quintana Roo), and César Duarte (Chihuahua) fled or were arrested.

sources.name: Animal Político, Panama Papers Mexico investigation

events.title: 7.1 Earthquake Kills 369 — Civil Society Repeats 1985
events.detail: A 7.1 magnitude earthquake struck central Mexico on the anniversary of the 1985 earthquake (September 19), killing 369 people. As in 1985, civil society mobilized rapidly and efficiently, often outperforming official response. The earthquake refocused attention on government corruption in building standards and galvanized a wave of civic engagement that fed into the 2018 election campaign.

sources.name: CENAPRED (National Center for Disaster Prevention) official report

events.title: AMLO Wins with 53.2% — Morena Landslide
events.detail: Andrés Manuel López Obrador won Mexico's presidency on his third attempt with 53.2% of the vote — the largest presidential margin since PRI's managed elections of the 1980s. Ricardo Anaya (PAN-PRD coalition) received 22.3% and José Antonio Meade (PRI) 16.4%. Morena won a majority in both chambers of Congress. The result reflected accumulated anger over corruption, insecurity, and economic inequality.

events.title: AMLO Inaugurated — 'Fourth Transformation' Begins
events.detail: AMLO was inaugurated at the National Congress and then addressed a massive crowd at the Zócalo. He declared the beginning of Mexico's 'Cuarta Transformación' (Fourth Transformation), comparing his government to the three previous national transformations: the Independence, the Reform (Juárez), and the Revolution. He announced immediate salary cuts for top officials, elimination of presidential perks, and conversion of the presidential residence (Los Pinos) into a public cultural center.

sources.name: Presidencia de la República, Diario Oficial, December 1, 2018

events.title: NAIM Cancelled — New Airport Controversy
events.detail: Before his inauguration, AMLO organized a non-binding public consultation that voted 70% to cancel the partially-built New International Airport of Mexico (NAIM) at Texcoco — a $13 billion project 30% complete. International investors lost confidence, Standard & Poor's and Fitch downgraded Mexico's credit outlook. AMLO redirected funds to the Santa Lucía military airbase conversion. The decision cost an estimated $5–13 billion in sunk costs and penalties.

sources.name: Presidencia de la República, consultation results

events.title: 2021 Midterms — Morena Maintains Majority
events.detail: Morena and allies won 280 of 500 seats in the Chamber of Deputies, retaining a working majority but losing the two-thirds supermajority that would have allowed constitutional reforms. PAN and allies (Va por México) won 197 seats, a significant opposition recovery. The election was marred by unprecedented political violence — 102 political candidates or campaign workers killed. Morena retained state governments in Guerrero, Sinaloa, and Sonora.

events.title: INE Reform ('Plan A') — Institutional Controversy
events.detail: AMLO's government passed a reform reducing INE's budget, eliminating proportional representation seats, and changing how INE councillors were selected. Opposition parties, civil society, and the INE itself protested. On February 26, 2023, one million people marched in Mexico City to defend INE — the largest pro-democracy march since the democratic transition. The Supreme Court struck down key provisions as unconstitutional.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, February 28, 2022

events.title: Ovidio Guzmán 'El Ratón' Extradited to U.S.
events.detail: Ovidio Guzmán López, son of El Chapo, was extradited to the United States on drug trafficking charges. Mexico had failed to capture him in 2019 in the 'Culiacanazo' incident when the Army freed him after cartel violence. His second arrest in January 2023 triggered cartel-organized blockades and violence in Sinaloa and Culiacán. His extradition was seen as AMLO signaling cooperation with the U.S. ahead of the 2024 election.

sources.name: U.S. Department of Justice press release, July 2023

events.title: Judicial Reform Enacted — Judges to Be Elected
events.detail: Mexico's Congress passed a constitutional reform requiring all judges — including the Supreme Court — to be elected by popular vote starting in 2025. The Supreme Court's 11 ministers, 107 circuit court judges, and nearly 1,500 federal judges would face elections. The U.S. ambassador Ken Salazar condemned the reform as a threat to democracy; judges went on strike; and the Supreme Court declared it unconstitutional in parts, but Congress and Morena proceeded. It was the most significant change to Mexico's legal architecture since 1917.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación, September 15, 2024
SCJN ruling on judicial reform, October 2024

events.title: Claudia Sheinbaum Wins with 59.4% — First Female President
events.detail: Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo won Mexico's presidential election with 59.4% — the highest vote share in Mexican democratic history — defeating Xóchitl Gálvez (opposition coalition PAN-PRI-PRD) with 28.2% and Jorge Álvarez Máynez (Movimiento Ciudadano) with 10.4%. Morena also won 12 of 9 contested governorships and maintained its congressional majority. The election was Mexico's largest — 98 million registered voters.

events.title: Sheinbaum Inaugurated — First Female President of Mexico
events.detail: Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo was inaugurated as Mexico's 66th president — the first woman to hold the office in 200 years of Mexican independence. AMLO transferred the presidential sash in a ceremony at the Chamber of Deputies attended by 30 heads of state. Sheinbaum promised to continue the 4T while emphasizing scientific knowledge and sustainability. She received the Bastón de Mando from indigenous communities in a ceremony at the Zócalo.

sources.name: Presidencia de la República, official inauguration record, October 1, 2024

events.title: First Judicial Elections — Judges Voted By Citizens
events.detail: Mexico held its first-ever judicial elections in June 2025, with citizens voting on candidates for the Supreme Court, circuit courts, and federal judgeships. Critics argued the process was chaotic, dominated by Morena-allied candidates, and lacked meaningful vetting. The U.S. government issued travel advisories related to uncertainty about judicial independence. International legal bodies monitored with concern. The elections reshaped Mexico's judiciary in a matter unprecedented in modern democratic history.

events.title: Morena Governs 24 of 32 States
events.detail: By 2025, Morena and its allies governed 24 of Mexico's 32 states — a level of territorial dominance not seen since PRI's peak in the 1980s. PAN retained Guanajuato, Querétaro, Yucatán, and Aguascalientes. PRI had only Coahuila. The opposition's fragmentation and Morena's social program infrastructure drove the geographic consolidation. Critics warned of a new political monopoly; Morena argued it reflected genuine popular support.
